questions.title: Word Ladder
questions.problem_statement: A transformation sequence from word beginWord to word endWord using a dictionary wordList is a sequence of words where every adjacent pair differs by a single letter. Given two words, beginWord and endWord, and a dictionary wordList, return the number of words in the shortest transformation sequence. Return 0 if no sequence exists.
questions.input_format: First line beginWord and endWord. Second line N (size of dictionary). Third line N space-separated words.
questions.output_format: A single integer denoting the length of the shortest sequence.
questions.constraints:
1 <= N <= 5000
1 <= word length <= 10
All words have the same length and consist of lowercase English letters.
questions.sample_input:
hit cog
6
hot dot dog lot log cog
questions.explanation_of_example: One shortest transformation sequence is 'hit' -> 'hot' -> 'dot' -> 'dog' -> 'cog', which is 5 words long.

python.explanation: Breadth First Search (BFS) is ideal for finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs. For every character in the word, mutate it from a-z, check if it's in the dict, and add to queue. Remove from dict to prevent revisits.

java.explanation: Use BFS with string mutations. Level order traversal ensures the first time we hit the target word, it is strictly via the shortest path. Using a HashSet provides O(1) dictionary lookups.

questions.title: Check Anagram
questions.problem_statement: Check if two strings are anagrams of each other. Print 'Yes' if they are, else 'No'.
questions.input_format: Two space-separated strings S1 and S2 on a single line.
questions.output_format: Print 'Yes' or 'No'
questions.constraints:
1 <= |S1|, |S2| <= 10^5
Strings contain only lowercase English letters.
questions.explanation_of_example: Both strings have the exact same character frequencies.

python.code:
s1, s2 = input().split()
if len(s1) != len(s2):
    print('No')
else:
    freq = [0]*26
    for c in s1: freq[ord(c)-97] += 1
    for c in s2: freq[ord(c)-97] -= 1
    print('Yes' if all(x == 0 for x in freq) else 'No')
python.explanation: Count character frequencies of the first string, decrement using the second. If the frequency array is all zeros, they are anagrams.

java.explanation: Use a character frequency array of size 26. Increment for string 1, decrement for string 2. Check if array becomes purely zeros.

approach.brute_force: Sort both strings and compare them for equality (O(N log N))
approach.optimal: Frequency counting array since character set is fixed lowercase (O(N))

questions.pro_tip: For anagrams involving just the alphabet, fixed-size frequency arrays are faster than hash maps.
common_mistakes: Not checking if lengths are equal first
Using HashMap resulting in slower execution overhead
Sorting strings which is slower than linear time

questions.title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
questions.problem_statement: There is an integer array nums sorted in ascending order (with distinct values). It is rotated at an unknown pivot index. Given a target K, return its index. If not found, return -1.
questions.input_format: First line N and K. Second line N integers.
questions.output_format: Index of K, or -1.
questions.explanation_of_example: The array is rotated at pivot 3. Target 0 is at index 4.

python.explanation: Identify the sorted half (either left or right). Check if the target lies within the boundaries of the sorted half, then adjust binary search bounds accordingly.

questions.title: Course Schedule
questions.problem_statement: There are a total of numCourses courses you have to take, labeled from 0 to numCourses - 1. You are given an array prerequisites where prerequisites[i] = [a, b] indicates that you must take course b first if you want to take course a. Return 'Yes' if you can finish all courses. Otherwise, return 'No'.
questions.input_format: First line numCourses N and edges E. Next E lines contain pairs 'a b'.
questions.output_format: Print 'Yes' or 'No'.
questions.explanation_of_example: To take course 1 you should have finished course 0. It is possible.

python.code:
from collections import deque, defaultdict
n, e = map(int, input().split())
adj = defaultdict(list)
indegree = [0] * n
for _ in range(e):
    u, v = map(int, input().split())
    adj[v].append(u)
    indegree[u] += 1
q = deque([i for i in range(n) if indegree[i] == 0])
count = 0
while q:
    curr = q.popleft()
    count += 1
    for neighbor in adj[curr]:
        indegree[neighbor] -= 1
        if indegree[neighbor] == 0:
            q.append(neighbor)
print('Yes' if count == n else 'No')
python.explanation: Kahn's Algorithm for Topological Sort. Compute in-degrees. Queue nodes with 0 in-degree. Process them, reducing neighbors' in-degrees. If total processed nodes equals total courses, graph is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG).

java.explanation: Use BFS to perform Topological Sorting. If there is a cycle in the prerequisites graph, nodes inside the cycle will never reach an indegree of 0, so the processed count will fall short of N.

approach.brute_force: Backtrack and trace all paths checking for repeated nodes (O(N!))
approach.optimal: Kahn's Algorithm (BFS) or Cycle Detection via DFS (O(V+E))

questions.pro_tip: Any problem involving 'prerequisites', 'dependencies', or 'order of tasks' is universally a Topological Sort / Graph Cycle Detection problem.
common_mistakes: Confusing edge direction (should be from prerequisite to target)
Not accounting for disconnected components in the graph
Using simple DFS without distinguishing between 'currently visiting' and 'fully visited' states, causing false cycle detections

questions.title: Next Greater Element
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of N integers, find the next greater element for each element of the array. The next greater element of an element is the first strictly greater element present to its right. If none exists, output -1 for that element.
questions.input_format: First line N. Second line N space-separated integers.
questions.output_format: N space-separated integers representing the next greater elements.
questions.explanation_of_example: Next greater to 1 is 3. To 3 is 4. To 2 is 4. To 4 there is none (-1).

python.code:
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
ans = [-1] * n
stack = []
for i in range(n - 1, -1, -1):
    while stack and stack[-1] <= arr[i]:
        stack.pop()
    if stack:
        ans[i] = stack[-1]
    stack.append(arr[i])
print(*ans)
python.explanation: Iterate from right to left using a monotonic stack. Pop elements smaller than current, the top of the stack is the next greater element. Push current element.

java.explanation: Traverse backwards. Keep a stack strictly monotonically decreasing. If elements in stack are smaller than current, they are useless, so pop them.

approach.brute_force: Nested loops, for each element traverse to the right to find the first greater element (O(N^2))
approach.optimal: Monotonic Stack traversal from right to left (O(N))

questions.pro_tip: Every element is pushed and popped at most once, making the while loop's amortized time complexity O(1).
common_mistakes: Using an O(N^2) double loop causing Time Limit Exceeded
Traversing left to right without storing array indices in the stack
Not popping elements that are equal (problem demands strictly greater)

questions.title: N-Queens (Count Solutions)
questions.problem_statement: The n-queens puzzle is the problem of placing n queens on an n x n chessboard such that no two queens attack each other. Given an integer n, return the number of distinct solutions to the n-queens puzzle.
questions.input_format: A single integer N.
questions.output_format: A single integer denoting the number of solutions.
questions.explanation_of_example: There are exactly two distinct solutions for a 4x4 chessboard.

python.code:
n = int(input())

def backtrack(row, diagonals, anti_diagonals, cols):
    if row == n:
        return 1
    solutions = 0
    for col in range(n):
        curr_diagonal = row - col
        curr_anti_diagonal = row + col
        if col in cols or curr_diagonal in diagonals or curr_anti_diagonal in anti_diagonals:
            continue
        cols.add(col)
        diagonals.add(curr_diagonal)
        anti_diagonals.add(curr_anti_diagonal)
        solutions += backtrack(row + 1, diagonals, anti_diagonals, cols)
        cols.remove(col)
        diagonals.remove(curr_diagonal)
        anti_diagonals.remove(curr_anti_diagonal)
    return solutions

print(backtrack(0, set(), set(), set()))
python.explanation: Use a backtracking function that places queens row by row. Maintain hash sets for columns, main diagonals (row - col), and anti-diagonals (row + col) to check for attacks in O(1) time.

java.explanation: Recursive backtracking evaluating row by row. Three HashSets track occupied columns and diagonals dynamically, providing constant time lookups for validity checks.

approach.brute_force: Generate all possible placements of N queens and validate each grid layout (O(N^N))
approach.optimal: Backtracking with mathematically mapped HashSets for diagonals (O(N!))

questions.pro_tip: For any cell (r, c), cells on the same main diagonal share the same difference (r - c), and cells on the same anti-diagonal share the same sum (r + c).
common_mistakes: Iterating through the entire board to check for queen threats instead of using mathematical sets
Failing to backtrack (remove from sets) after a recursive call returns
Attempting to solve for N > 12 where mathematical optimizations (like bitwise operators) are required to avoid TLE

questions.title: Regular Expression Matching

python.explanation: DP formulation where `dp[i][j]` maps `s[0..i]` to `p[0..j]`. The '*' operator looks back 2 positions for zero-occurrence (`dp[i][j-2]`), or checks if previous pattern character matches current string character to continue mapping (`dp[i-1][j]`).

approach.brute_force: Backtracking algorithm trying all possible regex length expansions
approach.optimal: Dynamic Programming matching subproblems recursively via 2D array

questions.title: Burst Balloons
questions.problem_statement: You are given n balloons, indexed from 0 to n - 1. Each balloon is painted with a number on it represented by an array nums. You are asked to burst all the balloons. If you burst the ith balloon, you will get nums[i - 1] * nums[i] * nums[i + 1] coins. If i - 1 or i + 1 goes out of bounds of the array, then treat it as if there is a balloon with a 1 painted on it. Return the maximum coins you can collect.
questions.input_format: First line N. Second line N space-separated integers.
questions.output_format: A single integer denoting max coins.

python.code:
n = int(input())
nums = [1] + list(map(int, input().split())) + [1]
n = len(nums)
dp = [[0] * n for _ in range(n)]
for length in range(2, n):
    for left in range(n - length):
        right = left + length
        for i in range(left + 1, right):
            coins = nums[left] * nums[i] * nums[right]
            dp[left][right] = max(dp[left][right], coins + dp[left][i] + dp[i][right])
print(dp[0][n - 1])
python.explanation: Interval DP. Instead of thinking 'which balloon to burst first', think 'which balloon is burst LAST'. Pad boundaries with 1s. Evaluate all subset ranges for maximum accumulated yields.

java.explanation: Padding array with 1 at both boundaries helps eliminate out-of-bounds checks. Solve dynamically over ranges of increasing lengths. DP state considers element `i` as the absolute last one to burst in current segment.

approach.brute_force: Recursively check all permutations of popping order taking O(N!)
approach.optimal: Interval DP utilizing reversed process thinking (O(N^3))

questions.pro_tip: Working backwards ('divide and conquer') is the only way to establish independent subproblems. The balloons are linked, but guessing the LAST balloon decouples the left and right subarrays.
common_mistakes: Applying a greedy solution bursting balloons linearly, missing optimized complex setups
Attempting to solve forward, creating dependent overlapping subproblems that break DP logic
Array boundary exception when handling limits on unpadded arrays

questions.title: Palindrome Partitioning Min Cuts
questions.problem_statement: Given a string s, partition s such that every substring of the partition is a palindrome. Return the minimum cuts needed for a palindrome partitioning of s.
questions.input_format: A single string s.
questions.output_format: A single integer denoting the minimum cuts.
questions.constraints:
1 <= s.length <= 2000
s consists of lowercase English letters only.
questions.explanation_of_example: The palindrome partitioning ["aa","b"] requires exactly 1 cut.

python.code:
s = input().strip()
n = len(s)
if n == 0:
    print(0)
    exit()
cuts = [0] * n
pal = [[False] * n for _ in range(n)]
for i in range(n):
    min_cut = i
    for j in range(i + 1):
        if s[j] == s[i] and (i - j <= 2 or pal[j + 1][i - 1]):
            pal[j][i] = True
            min_cut = 0 if j == 0 else min(min_cut, cuts[j - 1] + 1)
    cuts[i] = min_cut
print(cuts[n - 1])
python.explanation: DP logic combining palindrome verification and cut tracking. If `s[j:i]` is a palindrome, the minimum cuts at `i` would be the minimum cuts at `j-1` plus 1.

java.explanation: A dual DP approach. One 2D matrix caches whether substring i..j is a palindrome. The 1D cuts array determines the lowest possible cut required up to index i using the cache.

approach.brute_force: Backtracking checking all partitions taking O(2^N * N)
approach.optimal: Dynamic Programming with Palindrome Caching taking O(N^2)

questions.pro_tip: Checking palindromes linearly inside the DP loop makes the complexity O(N^3). Validating palindromes via a nested 2D Boolean array drops the lookup to O(1) saving massive runtime overhead.
common_mistakes: Applying a greedy approach making cuts at the earliest palindrome bounds which misses optimal combinations
Validating palindromes with linear iteration driving time limit exceeded
Returning the number of substrings instead of the number of cuts (cuts = substrings - 1)

questions.title: Network Delay Time
questions.problem_statement: You are given a network of n nodes, labeled from 1 to n. You are given times, a list of travel times as directed edges times[i] = (u, v, w), where u is the source node, v is the target node, and w is the time it takes for a signal to travel from source to target. We will send a signal from a given node k. Return the minimum time it takes for all the n nodes to receive the signal. If it is impossible, return -1.
questions.input_format: First line Nodes N, Edges E, Starting Node K. Next E lines contain 'u v w'.
questions.output_format: A single integer denoting the minimum time.
questions.explanation_of_example: Signal goes from 2->1 (time 1) and 2->3 (time 1). Then from 3->4 (time 1). Total time max(1, 1+1) = 2.

python.code:
import heapq
from collections import defaultdict
n, e, k = map(int, input().split())
graph = defaultdict(list)
for _ in range(e):
    u, v, w = map(int, input().split())
    graph[u].append((v, w))
pq = [(0, k)]
dist = {}
while pq:
    d, node = heapq.heappop(pq)
    if node in dist: continue
    dist[node] = d
    for neighbor, weight in graph[node]:
        if neighbor not in dist:
            heapq.heappush(pq, (d + weight, neighbor))
print(max(dist.values()) if len(dist) == n else -1)
python.explanation: Dijkstra's Algorithm. Use a Min-Heap (Priority Queue) to always expand the shortest path first. The total delay is the maximum shortest-path across all nodes. If a node is unreachable, the dictionary length will be less than N.

java.explanation: Implement Dijkstra's Algorithm via a PriorityQueue comparing cumulative path weights. Track visited nodes and their minimum resolved distances. Time is bounded by the longest shortest-path.

approach.brute_force: DFS mapping every possible path to every node keeping track of the minimums (O(N!))
approach.optimal: Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm (O(E log V))

questions.pro_tip: Because edge weights are non-negative, Dijkstra's algorithm is the fastest and most reliable method to compute single-source shortest paths globally.
common_mistakes: Failing to check if a node has already been relaxed (visited) resulting in infinite cycles
Adding the edge weight to the PriorityQueue instead of the cumulative path distance
Using 0-based indexing when the problem specifies 1-based labels

questions.title: Find Max and Min in Array
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of size N, find the maximum and minimum elements in the array.
questions.input_format: First line contains N. Second line contains N space-separated integers.
questions.output_format: Print max and min space-separated.
questions.explanation_of_example: The maximum element is 9 and the minimum is 1.

python.code:
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
max_val = arr[0]
min_val = arr[0]
for num in arr:
    if num > max_val: max_val = num
    if num < min_val: min_val = num
print(f"{max_val} {min_val}")
python.explanation: Initialize max and min with the first element. Iterate through the array updating max and min accordingly.

java.explanation: Read input dynamically without storing the whole array to save space. Update max and min for each input.

approach.brute_force: Sort the array and take first and last elements
approach.optimal: Linear scan comparing each element with current max and min

questions.pro_tip: You can process elements in pairs to reduce the number of comparisons by half.
common_mistakes: Initializing max to 0 instead of INT_MIN
Initializing min to 0 instead of INT_MAX
Sorting the array which takes O(N log N) time
similar_problems: Kth largest element in an array

questions.title: Pyramid Star Pattern
questions.problem_statement: Print a full pyramid star pattern of N rows.
questions.input_format: A single integer N.
questions.output_format: Pyramid pattern using stars and spaces.
questions.explanation_of_example: Row 1: 2 spaces, 1 star. Row 2: 1 space, 3 stars. Row 3: 0 spaces, 5 stars.

python.explanation: For each row i, print spaces equal to N-i-1 and stars equal to 2*i+1.

java.explanation: Nested loops for spaces followed by nested loops for stars. Calculation based on row index.

approach.brute_force: Nested loops formatting spaces and stars
approach.optimal: Nested loops formatting spaces and stars

questions.pro_tip: Notice the mathematical relation: stars = 2 * row_index + 1, spaces = total_rows - row_index - 1.
common_mistakes: Printing spaces after the stars
Incorrect math for number of spaces
Using even numbers of stars making pyramid lopsided
